metadata.project: Portable Waste Sorting Information for Seattle
metadata.source: Seattle Public Utilities — Where Does It Go?
metadata.access_method: downloadable static dataset or read-only API

quality_checks: each disposal stream must have a matching disposal condition when available
source_reference must link back to SPU guidance
security_controls: public records are distributed as read-only data
source_reference supports auditing and unauthorized-change detection
schema validation detects malformed or corrupted records
version metadata prevents accidental use of outdated structures

disposal_conditions.recycling: Empty aerosol cans that had non-hazardous materials in them can be put in the recycling ONLY if the can is completely emptied of contents and pressure.
disposal_conditions.garbage: • Empty hazardous material aerosol cans. • Partially full cans with non-hazardous content.
disposal_conditions.hazardous: Bring cans that contain hazardous materials like paint, solvents, bear spray, or other flammables to a household hazardous waste facility . No fee. Hazardous products are usually labeled with the words Danger, Poison, Caution, or Warning.

items.explanation: AEROSOL CANS may go in the garbage but only if they are empty or contain non-hazardous material.  Take cans with hazardous materials like paint, solvents, or other flammables to a household hazardous waste facility for free. For facility hours and location go to hazwastehelp.org.

disposal_conditions.hazardous: Batteries can't go in the garbage because they contain materials harmful to people, animals, and the environment.
disposal_conditions.dropoff: • Bring batteries to a household hazardous waste facility or city transfer station , where the materials will be reclaimed and recycled. No fee. • Accepted batteries include alkaline, button, rechargeable, and lead-acid batteries. • Separate batteries from devices. • Tape the ends of each individual household battery • Damaged and/or recalled batteries are NOT accepted at transfer stations. You can take them to household hazardous waste facility for free. • Call2Recycle is a free take-back program for rechargeable batteries. You can find free drop off locations or shop for mail-back packaging options. They accept: • Rechargeable batteries • Cellphones and cellphone batteries • E-Bike batteries (participating brands) • 1-800-RECYCLE is both a hotline and an online tool that connects you or your business to recycling services across Washington. This tool can help you find where in your community you can recycle everything from used appliances to leftover paint. Fees may apply.
disposal_conditions.special: Submit a request online or call (206) 684-3000 to schedule a pickup from your residence. The materials collected will be reclaimed and recycled. Fees apply.

items.explanation: Do not put batteries in your curbside trash or recycling bins. This includes Alkaline, Lithium, Lithium-Ion, Rechargeable, Computer, and Button batteries as well as products that contain batteries that cannot be easily removed. It is important to keep batteries out of your bins because they can cause fires in trucks and transfer stations. Batteries can be correctly disposed of in multiple ways. Batteries can be dropped off at a city transfer station or Hazardous Household Waste facility for free. You can also submit a special item pickup online or by calling 2066843000 to be picked up at your home. Fees apply for this service. You may also call 1800Recycle or go to call2recycle.com for additional drop off locations.

disposal_conditions.recycling: • Clean and dry aluminum foil should be balled up and larger than 3 inches • Aluminum food pans • Foil and trays must be empty, clean, and dry
disposal_conditions.garbage: • Dirty or food-covered aluminum foil or food pans • Wine bottle foil

items.explanation: ALUMINUM FOIL can be recycled. It needs to be clean and larger than 3 inches.  Dirty foil and little pieces go in the garbage but don't ball it up.

disposal_conditions.recycling: • Pieces of broken bottles and jars 3 inches or larger
disposal_conditions.garbage: • All pieces of broken drinking glasses or wine glasses • Pieces of broken bottles and jars smaller than 3 inches • All broken kitchenware glass or baking glass such as Pyrex • All broken frosted glass • Broken aquarium glass

items.explanation: Broken bottles and jars can be recycled. Broken drinking glasses, window, other types of glass or pieces smaller than 3 inches go in the garbage.

disposal_conditions.recycling: • Cardboard and flattened cardboard boxes go in the recycling. You can leave labels and tape on the boxes. Remove any packing materials inside and dispose of properly. • Pet scratch pads made of cardboard are recyclable.
disposal_conditions.compost: • Cardboard with grease or oil stains (like pizza boxes) go in the food and yard waste cart. • Put cardboard that is mostly wet in the food and yard waste cart.
disposal_conditions.garbage: • Waxed cardboard like produce boxes goes in the garbage.
disposal_conditions.special: Cardboard that doesn't fit can go next to your cart on your recycling day. Bundle and set next to the cart, or put in a box or container next to your cart. Remove foam and packing materials. There is no charge for extra recycling.

items.explanation: CARDBOARD BOXES go in the recycling.  Flatten them before putting in recycling.  Greasy boxes, like pizza boxes, and wet cardboard go in the food and yard waste cart.
